input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system under study is a serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes, featuring permanent magnet synchronous motors for each joint and gear transmission mechanisms. Its operational domain involves complex manufacturing tasks such as precision machining, where high accuracy and dynamic performance are critical for industrial applications.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides live-data linking between the physical robot and its digital counterpart, enabling real-time monitoring and visualization through an integrated dashboard. Key services include continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction parameters to enhance motion accuracy, as well as predictive maintenance capabilities derived from sensor data analysis.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation utilizes Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies including RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT for system abstraction. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs model components, interfaces, and interconnections. Behavior is detailed using MontiArc Automaton, SysML state machines, MathWorks Simulink for simulation, and general-purpose programming languages like C++ and Java.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models encompass SysML for requirements, functional, logical, and product/technical layers; ADLs (ACME, AADL, MontiArc, SysML IBDs) for component and connector perspectives; behavioral models using MontiArc Automaton and SysML state machines; and integration with MathWorks Simulink, simulation tools, and general-purpose code (C++/Java). Data originates from physical object state via one-way data flow, with data management involving integration of heterogeneous models and stakeholder perspectives as per MBSE methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT).
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor time series data, including input current and output angle, are streamed via MQTT protocol.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: MQTT protocol is used for data streaming between physical and digital components.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) splits modeling into abstraction levels including requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layer, with methodologies such as RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT used to manage complexity across these phases.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process for digital shadows follows a four-step methodology: Problem identification, Asset and model analysis, Use or creation of calculations or models, and Data identification (Section II.C), with Section IV demonstrating application of this process in practical scenarios.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The digital shadow enables insights such as detecting friction loss and improving robot motion accuracy through analytics, with user interaction facilitated via dashboard visualization for decision support.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with industry standards including SysML [17], AADL [24], ACME [23], and MontiArc [25], [26] for architecture description languages, methodologies such as RFLP [18], OOSEM [20], SPES [21], and SMArDT [22] for system complexity management, and simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink [28] along with general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java) for detailed behavior modeling.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the specific robot model, its components, and its operational application as described in Section III. It correctly mentions the MABI MAX 100, its six axes, the permanent magnet synchronous motors, and the use of gear transmissions. It also correctly identifies the application domain as complex manufacturing tasks like machining. Supported by quotes: 'serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes', 'driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor', 'suitable for complex manufacturing tasks such as machining'.

The extraction correctly identifies live-data linking, dashboard visualization, and continuous multi-physics-aware friction identification to enhance motion accuracy, which are the primary contributions described in the Abstract and Introduction. However, it adds 'predictive maintenance capabilities' as a key service, which is not explicitly stated in the provided text (the text focuses on 'detection of friction loss' and 'motion accuracy'). Despite this minor unsupported addition, the rest is highly accurate. Supported by quotes: 'live-data linking as well as visualization in a dashboard', 'enable the continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction', 'enhance the robot’s motion accuracy'.

The extraction is highly specific and directly supported by Section II.A. It lists MBSE methodologies 'e.g., RFLP..., OOSEM, SPES, or SMArDT', ADLs 'ACME, AADL, MontiArc... or SysML IBDs', and behavior modeling tools 'MontiArc Automaton', 'MathWorks Simulink', and 'general-purpose languages like C++ or Java'.
